column7: message

I mean, I don't know of any religion that specifically calls black people inferior but if a religious nut was to use that as an excuse instead of realizing they are a product of their environment, then we are truly dealing with someone in denial. So someone using their religion as a way to justify their racism is just plain ignorant. Also, a business owner wouldn't make that mistake (today) for fear of backlash. It's business suicide. 

>In my opinion, they are the same thing and the business owner should have the right to run his business however he wants to, even if I think (s)he is wrong.

And I agree. But that doesn't mean the business owner won't suffer consequences. It's matter of 'is the money more important or my beliefs'? And some choose the latter, and lose their business in the meantime. I believe that's exactly what happened to the cake shop.

Not a bad answer, and certainly something I thought about before I responded.  The reason I didn't go there is because of the follow up question I anticipated.  What if someone's personal belief (and a tenant of their religion) is that black people are inferior and white people shouldn't do business with them? 

In my opinion, they are the same thing and the business owner should have the right to run his business however he wants to, even if I think (s)he is wrong.

column7:
somatotypes are a myth though, the variance in metabolic speed between two individuals of similar age, and height is ~ 200 calories, at the extreme ends of the scale, that's like 1 in 7 billion odds. Most people are well within smaller ranges of each other towards the middle of the bell curve. That is to say, it's safe to bet on your metabolism not being "highly efficient at storing fat" than the person next to you due to your 'german ancestry.' 

You need to count your calories and compare it to your actual TDEE, do the same for your brother, you'd be surprised how little he eats in comparison to his energy expenditure.

Actually you need to broaden it:

Most people lack discipline, at least in some areas.

Some of those people get fat.

One of the shittier things in my life was growing up next to a string bean brother.  We ate the same meals, had the same hobbies, but I was always struggling to avoid the chub and he could add in extra snacks while staying skinny as a rake.  Fucker.

So what you actually have is a false *negative* for lack of discipline.

Think of it like a Venn Diagram.  Take Disciplined People and thin people as the two main circles.  The actual overlap between them is much smaller than you think, and there are a hell of a lot of thin people who also lack discipline.

One of the main issues here though is that you've actually misunderstood 'discipline'.  Decision fatigue, as well as resistance fatigue, are very real things.

So you may find that Bob the chubby guy has very little discipline when it comes to diet and exercise. On the other hand, his mathematics homework is flawless.  He's spending his 'discipline energy' on something else.

Meanwhile Steve the stereotypical jock has no discipline when it comes to his schoolwork, but gets up an hour early to go for a run and squeeze in a body weight routine.  Lazy with schoolwork, disciplined with physical fitness.

Now, different people tire out more quickly than others, but imagine for a moment just how many other factors intervene.  Are you an introvert stuck hanging around loads of people? You'll struggle to have anything left in you after resisting your desire to leave all day.  Likewise if you're an extrovert who has to sit still and be quiet all day you're going to have your own struggles.

So be careful when you talk about discipline, because it's not so simple or binary.

But also be careful when you consider weight issues, because my brother and I have similar levels of discipline, but he's still skinny.  His lack of discipline is hidden from view.

column7:
>>Harassment includes offensive verbal comments related to gender, sexual orientation, disability, physical appearance, body size, race, religion, sexual images in public spaces, deliberate intimidation, stalking, following, harassing photography or recording, sustained disruption of talks or other events, inappropriate physical contact, and unwelcome sexual attention

So, where in that long list of things do you cover "Telling an immature joke to your friend/coworker?

They weren't talking gender or sexual orientation.

They weren't talking about a disability.

They were not making reference to someone's physical appearance

Not body size, race, religion or sexual images. and the rest is all moot.

They made a penis joke, about hardware. Mind you fictional hardware. Which frankly in legalese isn't covered by the aforementioned description of harassment. The statement was not made at anyone. It was a joke that was overheard by a bystander who was either eavesdropping, or being malicious.

>>had no hand in actually getting them fired 

Most people would have turned around and asked the individuals to stop. Putting them on blast via twitter was a calculated endeavor.

>> and then was fired herself after misogynistic assholes on the internet DDoS'ed her company and sent her death threats?

Being fired for costing your company money is hardly misogynistic. After all, everyone fired in this situation was essentially let go for that one singular reason.

> People have become trained to avoid saying stigmatized words for fear of having their lives ruined after events like this.

What, like the woman who tweeted it with the express purpose of notifying the event administrators that [the event's code of conduct was being violated](https://github.com/python/pycon-code-of-conduct), had no hand in actually getting them fired (and actually [didn't want the firing to happen](http://www.feministe.us/blog/archives/2013/03/21/standing-with-adria/)), and then was fired herself after misogynistic assholes on the internet DDoS'ed her company and sent her death threats? Yeah, PC culture at work, for sure.

"@central-spore, wow, you're really grasping at straws here. You think you're some kind of hero, defending the poor, misunderstood woman who got fired for making a penis joke? Newsflash: if you're going to make a joke about hardware, don't do it in a professional setting, especially if it's a joke that's going to offend someone. And don't try to spin it as 'not about a disability' or 'not about a woman' - it's about being considerate of others. And as for the woman who got fired, maybe she should've thought twice before tweeting about it. You're not exactly helping her cause by making it all about PC culture. Just saying."

"@critical-geyser, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topic. While it's understandable to want to consider others in a professional setting, the original post raises a valid question about the definition of harassment. The code of conduct mentioned does list a wide range of behaviors, but it's worth exploring the nuances of what constitutes harassment in different contexts. Let's avoid making assumptions or jumping to conclusions about the individuals involved. If you'd like to discuss this further, I'm here to facilitate the conversation."

column7:
I was one of those kids.  My grandparents all built up their lives through honest hard work and good planning from almost nothing.  My parents were responsible enough to build on what their parents had done and not lose it all.

I have grown up ashamed of the situation I was born into.  I was and disconnected from my past because I believed rich people were evil, because everyone was always saying they were.  I found it difficult to connect with my peers because of our different financial situations.  I found it difficult to cope when I realized I wasn't better and my position made me uncomfortable.  Nobody talks about my problems or has compassion for them.  I've been made fun of my whole life because I was a "trust funder", instead of being supported in becoming the best I could be for my people and encouraged to take the social responsibility of what I'd been given seriously.

I can't speak for other families, but in general I've come to believe rich people are not evil, they are often just financially very responsible.

Do you have zero respect or compassion for me?  Do you believe I should be neglected?

If nobody deserves special privileges because their ancestors had bad luck, why do some deserve special contempt because their ancestors had good luck?

> but to say that they deserve special privileges because their ancestors had bad luck is asinine

So what about the white kids with rich parents who don't have to work a day in their life? They inherit property, land, have top dollar educations? Are they surviving because they're the "fittest"?

"oh great, another rich kid whining about how hard it is to be them. Newsflash, celeste-convection: your life is still a cakewalk compared to mine. I'm the one who's been struggling to make ends meet, working multiple jobs just to get by. And you're over here complaining about how hard it is to be a trust funder? @celeste-convection, get some perspective. Your problems are not my problems."

column7:
My understanding is that cultural appropriation is a neutral term. It's an academic description of cultural borrowing, which is neither good nor bad. 

Its negative connotation comes when a colonial culture takes something in a way that trivializes and destroys its meaning. Hipsters who wear feather headresses are disrespecting a symbol that still has meaning and cultural import to existing Native American cultures, for example. It's sort of like going around wearing a fake purple heart. The fact that we'd treat the faked heart as an issue of stolen valor and think that the headdress is just hipsters hipstering says something about the relistionship between cultures. 

Wearing a feather headdress you haven't earned is a disrespectful and destructive act, in other words, and an example of negative cultural appropriation, Applying a similar lens to other examples should help you navigate other situations sensibly .

column7:
What's the solution?  Realistically, you can't just let anyone in a skirt & wig into the women's restroom with women & little girls, b/c there is no way to safeguard against people who are predators & simply using a loophole to get into women's restrooms.  And, the cost to build gender neutral restrooms nationwide would be astronomical, and a huge burden on businesses.  I sympathize with transgender people.  I can't imagine a more emotionally agonizing fate than being born into the wrong body.  But, we're talking about a very small percentage of the US population that are impacted.  From the reading I've done, it seems most experts in the field quote 1:30,000 MTF transexualism and 1:100,000 FTM transexualism.  The most extreme #s I've seen used are 1:2,500 for both.  In other words, most people in the field say 1 out of every 30,000 men are transsexuals living as or wanting to live as a woman, and 1 out of every 100,000 are women living as or wanting to live as a man.  That's .0033% of the adult male population, and .001% of the adult female population as median population figures for transexualism, with a most extreme prediction of 1 out of 2,500 adults, for a percentage of .04%.  That means most experts predict there are 3,962 adult males & 1,250 females living with transexualism, with the most extreme prediction being 98,000.  

Now, none of this is detailed to say there aren't enough transexuals for the issue to matter, but rather, to at least paint a picture of the scope of the problem.  Do we pass laws and for accommodations for every person with a rare disorder?  I'm not saying "no", but it's impossible to accommodate all people all the time.  There's simply a finite amount of time & resources.  So what's the solution?  I'd certainly love to hear ideas.
Its not a loophole. Harrasment and assault are still illegal. As soon as our hypothetical perv starts peering unto the stalls they're breaking the law. You can call security. You can call police

@braised-helper, I'm so disappointed in your response. You're basically saying that trans people are a tiny minority and therefore don't deserve equal rights. That's not how it works. The fact that there are only a few trans people in the country doesn't make their struggles any less valid. And what about the fact that many trans people are misgendered or harassed just for existing? You're not considering the emotional toll of constantly being told you don't belong or that you're a "loophole". @hushed-tetrad, I appreciate your response, but it's not that simple. What about people who are trans and don't feel comfortable calling security or the police? What about those who are trans and don't have the privilege of being able to "just call security"?

"@stringy-sopapillas, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on finding solutions. @braised-helper, your point about the cost and feasibility of implementing gender-neutral restrooms nationwide is valid, but @stringy-sopapillas raises a crucial point about the emotional toll of marginalization and harassment on the trans community. @hushed-tetrad, your suggestion to call security or the police is a good one, but @stringy-sopapillas highlights the limitations of this approach for those who may not feel safe or empowered to do so. Let's continue the conversation with empathy and understanding."

column7:
> Making customers feel like shit if they can't tip or if they don't tip enough.

So don't eat there.  That's like saying that it shouldn't be okay to charge $30 for a steak because not everyone can afford it.

> Tipping shouldn't give you better service. If you receive good service, that just means they did their job.

It doesn't.  It **rewards** better service.  You tip at the end.  

> Would it not be better to charge the customer 10$ for that burger and pay the waiter a fair salary?

So in either scenario, you paid $10, right?  So what difference does it make to you?  In the tipping scenario, your entire $2 went straight to the server.  If you funnel it through the business, you really think that the entire $2 increase is going to the server?  Of course not.  The owner is going to pocket some of it on the way.  Now you paid more AND the server got less.  

When you eliminate tipping and roll it into the price of the food, the ONLY person that wins is the business owner.  You lose, and the server loses. 

Trust me, having been a server for a long time, none of us are complaining about the pay.  I cleared $25/hr **easy** on a good night.  Find me a place that's going to pay me $25/hr as a "fair wage."

>So don't eat there.  That's like saying that it shouldn't be okay to charge $30 for a steak because not everyone can afford it.

But they *can* eat there. Because they just ate there. Tipping isn't mandatory. If someone can buy a $30 steak but doesn't/can't tip, that's perfectly acceptable.

@organic-texture, wow, you're really grasping at straws here. You think you're so clever with your "they can eat there" argument, but let's be real, if someone can afford a $30 steak but can't tip, they're still contributing to the problem. It's not about being "mandatory" to tip, it's about basic human decency. And by the way, if you're so concerned about people not being able to afford it, maybe you should be advocating for a living wage for servers instead of just lecturing customers.

column7:
> practicing active deception

Is the deception itself the problem then?

>They have gone to extreme lengths to make themselves appear to be something physically that they know (rightly or wrongly) many people will not consider them to be.

So to what degree should we apply this standard? Should a person who appears to be fully of one race but shares a quarter of a race that some person out there might find objectionable to have slept with?

The difference is that (arguably from many people's points of view) a post op trans person is practicing active deception.  They have gone to extreme lengths to make themselves appear to be something physically that they know (rightly or wrongly) many people will not consider them to be.

column7:
At my high school, getting additional funding for the science program was *directly contingent* on students winning science awards and being accepted to B.Sc-granting degree programs. There's no science proficiency test we could all take, so grants flowed from these markers of success.

Moreover, you'll note that high schools, regardless of region, publish the graduation and university acceptance rates of their students. These numbers are significant and awards are a way of boosting them.

I think you need to view high school awards in context here. They're meant to give kids opportunity. The guy who started the robotics club and took 2 AP classes and got an internship? He's *fine*. He's set. He can go to any college of his choosing, barring any massive screwups or his SAT results catching fire. 

Coding Girl? We just don't know. Maybe you're underplaying her achievements, but then again, perhaps the only thing on her resume is coding club and that award. Now, she's given a shot at better schools. Your school can brag about sending *both* Robotics Guy and Coding Girl to Ivy Leagues. 

Is that fair to Robotics Guy? Probably not. But it can do some great things for your school, in terms of reputation and funding. I get the feeling that you're ascribing to sexism what is better explained by academic politics.

@complete-taping, I'm not saying it's all about sexism, but what about the girl who's been coding since she was 10? She's got a real shot at those top colleges, and it's not just about the award. She's got skills, dedication, and a passion that Robotics Guy might not even understand. And yeah, it's great for the school's rep, but what about the girl who's been working hard in the shadows? She deserves recognition too, not just the one who's been making noise.

@braised-helper, @complete-taping, @muted-bobtail, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topic. While we appreciate the passion and perspectives shared, let's avoid making assumptions about individual students or their achievements. The goal is to understand the context of the high school's funding system and its potential impact on students.

> They're trying to make it look like the users are organically supporting Hillary. It very much appears to be deceptive propaganda funded by a superPAC and Reddit is either complicit in this or is being paid for it.

My comment was addressing this aspect directly. You can believe that the opposition is being censored and I haven't argued against that. But the support that is there is genuine. How dominant that support is may be inorganic, but it is real. 

> and it started pretty much overnight which coincided when the Hillary superPAC Correct The Record got their funding.

This is completely false. We've known about CTR for months, since the primaries. In fact, they got their budget increase back in July, when Reddit still thought she was the devil, in Bernie or bust mode. The timeline doesn't add up at all.

column7:
We're not talking about downvoted comments here. We're talking about *deleted* comments. 

That's not organic. That's systematic suppression and censorship in a huge subreddit that is masquerading as a neutral ground for discussion.

And it's obviously coordinated. Every thread in r/politics is like this, and it started pretty much overnight which coincided when the Hillary superPAC Correct The Record got their funding.

"Oh great, another conspiracy theory. You think Reddit is in the pocket of Hillary's superPAC? That's rich. You're just mad because your precious Bernie got roasted in the comments. Newsflash: people actually like Hillary, and they're not afraid to say it. And yeah, I'm sure it's just a coincidence that the pro-Hillary comments started pouring in after CTR got funded. I mean, it's not like people were just waiting for a green light to start spouting their opinions. @succulent-audience, you're so gullible it's adorable."

@succulent-audience, @rounded-camera, @progressive-rowhouse, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topic. Avoid making accusations or personal attacks. If you have concerns about moderation or the platform, please report them to the Reddit staff.

column7:
>So is your view, more specifically, that we should abolish the FDA, and not allow any regulation on drugs, including proof that they do what they claim to do and that they contain the ingredients they provide?

Almost. Yes we should abolish the FDA. No we should not get rid of false advertising laws. Companies should not be allowed to lie about the contents or nature their products, as that would be an inherent violation of a buyer-seller agreement. We do not need the FDA specifically to enforce such laws.

As for the rest. Yes I agree medicine is extremely complex. Yes I agree that it is extremely difficult for an individual consumer to make well-informed choices on his own. However, government regulation should not be the answer for two reasons: 1. You assume that information can't exist without government. It can. Private companies can, and already do, provide reputable information regarding almost any field. 2. The government doesn't not have your best interests in mind. The government does not have the same incentives to please customers as private companies do. If private companies do a bad job at providing good information, they lose out on money. If the government does a bad job, the consequences for politicians are minimal.

So is your view, more specifically, that we should abolish the FDA, and not allow any regulation on drugs, including proof that they do what they claim to do and that they contain the ingredients they claim to have? Because it sounds like that is your view.

The problem isn't that people are stupid, the problem is that medicine is *extremely complex.* It is massively beneficial for society to have regulations and standards in place to ensure that people seeking healthcare are given advice by somebody certified to do so, and that the medicines they receive are safe, effective, and contain what they say they do. To be a "smart" consumer of medicine without regulations in place would require years of study in medicine, chemistry (to characterize the mystery drugs you are sold), and pharmacology, along with a ton of luck. That's... extremely unreasonable, compared to having a robust system in place so that people can actually receive quality healthcare.

And the same thing goes for almost all regulation! It's extremely difficult for an individual consumer to make well-informed choices in our highly complex, highly-specialized world, especially when deregulated industries stand to make the most profit from exploiting that lack of knowledge. Regulation allows consumers to have a baseline level of safety when making choices and prevent situations where its impossible to know if *any* of the products you're being sold are of acceptable quality.

E: To be clear, I am not saying that I support the *specific* regulations currently in place on drugs in all cases, but that I think it's extremely naïve to be against *the idea* of regulating drugs, or to believe that the only purpose of regulation is to protect idiots*. Regulation is extremely important, its the degree and the details that matter.

*Also, like... idiots are people too. Even if a given regulation only prevented preying on dumb people, as long as its effective that's still a good thing? I don't really get the idea that people deserve to be exploited or taken advantage of if they aren't smart enough to figure it out.

@rounded-camera, wow, you're really good at copying and pasting. I'm impressed. But let's get to the meat of the issue. column7:
Let's dispel with this notion that police are targeting and murdering innocent black people apropos of nothing. If you're a black person with no history of criminal activity, you have a near-zero chance of being the victim of police brutality. 

In all of the high-profile cases of police killing unarmed black suspects (Eric Garner, Michael Brown, Alton Sterling etc.), to a man the individual in question had a sordid history of violent felonies, was in the process of committing crimes at the time of their deaths, and actively and violently resisted arrest. I'd say that being a violent felon who is confrontational and belligerent to police increases your chances of being brutalized by police far more than your race or ethnicity.

I would argue that this has much more to do with the fact that it was captured on video and is pretty clearly an execution type killing. He was on the ground, restrained, and had a gun pointed at his head. If the police executed a Hispanic, Asian, etc. in this same style and it was caught on video, there would be similar uproar, albeit without the BLM hype machine behind it. 

Further, black oppression by the police is being highlighted more than white meth head deaths and anti-Asian and anti-Hispanic violence for pretty good reason. While I disagree with BLM's methods of drawing attention, the fact is that black people are much more heavily targeted and discriminated against by the police. 

You could make an (uninformed and context-ignorant) argument that black people commit more crime than those groups, so they're not being discriminated against, but you'd be missing the point. The point is that when a black person is stopped for the same crime as a white or Asian or Hispanic person, their treatment is handled much differently. Black people are much more likely to be rough housed, beaten, or killed for a minor stop than other minorities. 

Context: I am a young white guy who lives in the south, grew up pretty privileged, moderate leaning toward the right, well-educated, and it would be pretty difficult to accuse me of suffering from white guilt if you knew me personally - I personally put a significant portion of blame for black issues in America squarely on black culture. In other words, I'm exactly the type of person who you might see on the other side of this argument. This is probably the most obvious form of open racism and oppression the black community faces today, which is why it's so publicized - it's so easy to see.

Oh great, another "I'm a moderate right-leaning white guy who's not racist" trying to lecture us on racism. @magnetic-concentration, you're so brave to bring up the "violent felon" excuse, but let's be real, if Eric Garner was just selling loose cigarettes, he wouldn't have been choked to death. And Michael Brown was walking down the street, not committing a crime. And Alton Sterling was in his own store, not resisting arrest. column7:
>while a young rookie MP that doesn't even show up to report in her own portfolio and cites Twitter trends instead of actual data with a straight face in parliamentary justifications. This is but one example. In the face of her incompetence on so many levels and waste of resources, I know at least that she met one criteria when she was appointed to cabinet: she was a woman, and that was made an explicit job requirement for half of the ministers.

As a Canadian, which MP are you referring to?

>Why do you assume that the women appointed have no merit? Where are the women who are rookie MPs?

I won't bore you with political citations. I'm sure that if you're actually interested you could look up the Canadian cabinet yourself. It is dry stuff. In particular, I assume this because I read about my government daily as a hobby. I am continually upset that the electoral reform expert in the party is off playing diplomat in other countries, decidedly not his field of expertise, while a young rookie MP that doesn't even show up to report in her own portfolio and cites Twitter trends instead of actual data with a straight face in parliamentary justifications. This is but one example. In the face of her incompetence on so many levels and waste of resources, I know at least that she met one criteria when she was appointed to cabinet: she was a woman, and that was made an explicit job requirement for half of the ministers.

To be clear, I do not think women are all incompetent. We have some strong female cabinet ministers as well, and the other two major parties have strong female candidates running for leadership. They demonstrably can hire for merit and still have many women in cabinet. That just adds to the absurdity of explicitly embracing affirmative action in the appointment process.

column7: Might be way off here but Australia seems hard to compare to other countries, on a variety of issues, possibly due to the vast population density disparities and unique history? I honestly know very little other than Australia is an outlier in many areas of measurement when compared to other developed nations. Can't speak to the reasons so crucify me please! but I doubt it is just because "you do it the best" or the systems are so unique.

I just wanted to contest the lines argument a little. I'm Australian and while I make decent money (my wife and I earn comfortably 6 figures between us), I prefer a public healthcare system to a private one. I haven't had to interact much with healthcare here, but I've always found it to be very quick and easy to get an appointment with my GP, and on both occasions that I've needed a scan (once a neck ultrasound to check an enlarged lymph node, and once an mri for a knee injury) I was able to organise an anointment at a local clinic the next day on both occasions, and waited less than 10 minutes each time.
